data_collected: Camera content and camera roll settings
Interactions with AI at Meta and related metadata
Types of content viewed and interaction patterns
Time, frequency, and duration of activities
On-device behavior (e.g., mouse movement, foreground app status)
Information from partners, vendors, and third parties
third_party_sharing: Legal requests (law enforcement, government authorities, civil litigants)
Potential new owners (in case of business sale or transfer)
security_practices: Contractual rules for third-party data use and disclosure
user_rights: View and manage information via settings
red_flags: Manual review: Humans may access and review user information in some cases
Shadow tracking: Data is collected even if you don't have an account
Behavioral tracking: Monitoring mouse movements to distinguish humans from bots
Vague retention: Data kept on a 'case-by-case basis' depending on 'legitimate purposes'
Cross-company sharing: Extensive data pooling across all Meta Companies (Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp)
Off-platform tracking: Collection of 'Off-Meta activity' from third-party partners
Broad legal sharing: Data may be shared with civil litigants, not just law enforcement
justification: Meta's policy describes an exhaustive surveillance model that tracks users and non-users across devices, apps, and physical locations. The inclusion of human 'manual review' of data, tracking of granular behavior like mouse movements, and the sharing of data across its massive corporate ecosystem presents a high privacy risk. While user rights for deletion and access are provided, the sheer volume of 'optional' but highly invasive data collection and the use of vague retention periods contribute to a high score.
